title: DSAR fulfillment SLA tracker with verification evidence trail
exclusions: Does NOT detect incomplete returns (scope-gap); pair with UC-22.36.2 for erasure propagation.
evidence: Daily report 'dsar_sla_tracker' writes per-ticket status (received/verified/responded/fulfilled) to compliance_summary; Glass Table panel shows funnel with aging.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Art.15 right of access requires fulfilment 'without undue delay and in any event within one month' (Art.12(3)); SLA tracker measures this directly with verifiable timestamps.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that GDPR Art.15 (Right of access) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.36.1: DSAR fulfillment SLA tracker with verification evidence trail.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.36.1' running on catalogue-defined data sources (see UC detailedImplementation), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Art.12 governs transparent communication; the UC evidences that the response SLA was met and identifies the exceptions.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that GDPR Art.12 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.36.1: DSAR fulfillment SLA tracker with verification evidence trail.

compliance.assurance_rationale: §1798.100 gives consumers the right to request the categories/specific pieces of PI a business has collected; CCPA requires a 45-day response with one 45-day extension — the SLA tracker is direct evidence of compliance.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that CCPA/CPRA §1798.100 (Consumer right to know) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.36.1: DSAR fulfillment SLA tracker with verification evidence trail.

controlTest.positiveScenario: When a DSAR ticket crosses the 25-day mark without a 'verified' transition, the UC fires and lists the ticket_id, data subject token, and days_open.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Tickets within their SLA window, or tickets on a lawfully-extended clock (Art.12(3)) with an approved extension in the lookup, do NOT fire.

dataSources: See subcategory preamble. — supplemented by authentication logs, system audit trails, and configuration management database (CMDB) asset inventory exports
spl:
index=dsar sourcetype=onetrust:dsar OR sourcetype=servicenow:privacy earliest=-90d
| stats min(_time) as received max(eval(case(status="verified",_time))) as verified max(eval(case(status="fulfilled",_time))) as fulfilled by ticket_id subject_token
| eval days_open=round((coalesce(fulfilled,now())-received)/86400,1), sla_breach=if(isnull(fulfilled) AND days_open>25,1,0)
| where sla_breach=1
| table ticket_id subject_token received verified days_open
description: Produces per-ticket SLA evidence for every data-subject access / deletion / portability request. Tickets that approach the statutory response window are surfaced so the DPO can intervene; tickets that breached are logged to the evidence pack with clear root cause.
value: Replaces the common 'DPO exports a CSV from OneTrust' evidence pattern with a continuously-measured, tamper-evident record that directly supports auditor questions about Art.12/Art.15 response timeliness.
implementation: (1) Ingest the DSAR platform (OneTrust, TrustArc, ServiceNow Privacy) via its API; (2) join to an `extensions.csv` for legitimately extended tickets; (3) alert to DPO when breach is imminent; (4) emit KPI to ITSI privacy service. Indexes required: `index=dsar`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=onetrust:dsar`, `sourcetype=servicenow:privacy`.
visualization: Funnel (received → verified → responded → fulfilled), aging bar chart, single-value for '% of DSARs fulfilled within SLA (90d)'.
knownFalsePositives:
1. **Authorised compliance operations** — The compliance and governance team performs routine control testing, evidence gathering, and policy reviews that generate legitimate activity matching this use case's detection patterns. These operations are documented in the compliance programme calendar and should be excluded by the compliance-ops service account tag.

2. **Scheduled system maintenance windows** — Approved maintenance activities including patching, upgrades, and configuration changes produce expected control deviations during documented change windows that are pre-approved through the change advisory board (CAB).

3. **External audit and assessment activities** — During periodic audits (internal or external), assessors perform sampling, testing, and evidence collection activities that generate unusual access patterns covered by the engagement letter and audit notification.

4. **Third-party service provider operations** — Contracted service providers performing legitimate operations under signed agreements generate expected activity that may trigger monitoring rules during normal service delivery windows.

**Suppression mechanism:** Maintain a `compliance_exception_register` KV Store lookup containing approved maintenance windows, audit engagement periods, service provider identifiers, and compliance team service accounts. The correlation search joins this lookup and suppresses matches where `exception_status=approved` and the exception remains within its validity period.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Indexes**: `dsar` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `Art.15`. The control objective is: "Evidence that GDPR Art.15 (Right of access) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.36.1: DSAR fulfillment SLA tracker with verification evidence trail." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is bespoke for this UC — no single canonical TA parses every source the detection relies on. Route data into `dsar` under `onetrust:dsar`, `servicenow:privacy`, and document the ingestion path in the UC runbook so future engineers know where each field originates.


Four distinct operational considerations shape this detection's rollout:


• **Hint 1:** Ingest the DSAR platform (OneTrust, TrustArc, ServiceNow Privacy) via its API. Ingestion SLA drift is usually invisible until it breaks something — monitor the feed's freshness with a companion search that alerts when the newest event is older than the expected cadence.

• **Hint 2:** join to an `extensions.csv` for legitimately extended tickets. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 3:** alert to DPO when breach is imminent. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 4:** emit KPI to ITSI privacy service. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

The detection fires when `days_open > 25`. For "DSAR fulfillment SLA tracker with verification evidence trail," the aggregation is the evidence artifact itself — auditors will read these rows as the official record of GDPR control activity. This threshold is the operational heart of the control — set it too low and the signal drowns in noise; set it too high and real compliance deviations go undetected. The value `25` should never be treated as universal; it must be calibrated against your specific operational baseline.

Calibration procedure: before enabling this as a production alert, run the base search over the last 30 days with the threshold removed and compute distribution statistics:

```spl
<base search> earliest=-30d
| stats count by days_open
| stats avg(days_open) as mean, perc50(days_open) as p50, perc90(days_open) as p90, perc99(days_open) as p99, max(days_open) as hi
```

Set the production threshold between p90 and p99 depending on your organisation's alert fatigue tolerance. Document the chosen value in the control specification alongside the date of calibration and the sample size — auditors will ask how you arrived at `25` and the defensible answer is 'data, plus a documented review by the control owner,' not 'the template said so.'

The `min()` aggregation over the last 90 days is a deliberate choice. Compliance reports on sustained behaviour, not instantaneous spikes — a single noisy event crossing the threshold for one second should not page the on-call. If your organisation requires point-in-time sensitivity (e.g. real-time fraud detection), shorten the time window and aggregation span; for accountability reporting, widen them. Tune for the regulation's actual expectation, not engineering preference.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.36.1: DSAR fulfillment SLA tracker with verification evidence trai]
cron_schedule = 0 3 1 * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -90d
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.36.1,reg=GDPR
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=dsar sourcetype=onetrust:dsar earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `days_open`, `fulfilled`, `received`, `subject_token`, `ticket_id`, `verified`. Run:

```spl
index=dsar sourcetype=onetrust:dsar earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table days_open, fulfilled, received, subject_token, ticket_id, verified
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(d) Threshold calibration:** Before enabling as a production alert, run the base search with the threshold removed and profile the distribution of `days_open`:

```spl
<base search> earliest=-30d
| stats perc50(days_open) as p50 perc90(days_open) as p90 perc99(days_open) as p99 max(days_open) as hi
```

The current threshold `25` should sit between p90 and p99 for a balanced signal-to-noise ratio. If it's below p50, expect heavy alert volume; if above p99, expect under-detection.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.36.1: GDPR evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Funnel (`received → verified → responded → fulfilled`):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 2 — Aging Bar Chart (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 3 — Single-Value For '% Of Dsars Fulfilled Within Sla (90D)' (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**Too many or too few alerts:** The threshold is almost certainly miscalibrated for your estate — re-run the percentile calibration (Step 3d) on a representative 30-day window and pick a value between p90 and p99.

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**GDPR-specific failure modes:**

• Lawful basis drift: a detection that worked when the processing activity had consent as its lawful basis becomes misleading when the business team silently changes the RoPA (Record of Processing Activities) entry to 'legitimate interest.' Every GDPR compliance search should reference the RoPA entry ID in its comments and re-review the detection when the RoPA is revised — at minimum annually.

• Data Subject Rights timers: Article 12(3) requires response to access/erasure/portability requests within one month. Detections that measure DSR fulfilment must use business-day clocks (not wall-clock 30 days) for regulators that enforce the distinction; the threshold value in the search should align with the local Supervisory Authority's interpretation, not the ISO 8601 literal.

• Cross-border transfer fragility: Schrems II invalidated the EU-US Privacy Shield in 2020 and the EU-US Data Privacy Framework (2023) replaced it; any detection involving transfer monitoring must key off the current adequacy decision list, not a hardcoded country allowlist. Maintain `gdpr_adequacy_list.csv` and refresh when the EDPB updates the adequacy register.

• Breach notification 72-hour window (Art. 33): the clock starts at awareness, not at the forensically-confirmed moment of compromise. Detections must capture the first-awareness event (SOC alert acknowledgment, first ticket open, first email notification) — not just the incident-resolved timestamp — or the notification SLA metric will understate breaches that took 72h+1h to confirm.
grandmaExplanation: We help you see when data-subject requests are running late so privacy duties stay on track across systems.
